## Reflection

### Advanced R Programming

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Question 1: What I Learned from Functions (Dave Robinson)

- Functions reduce repetition\
- It's easier to use the function when repeating codes
- It helps save a lot of time when writing codes
- Helps reuse codes later easier
- Function matters because it will make debugging easier, and makes codes look more organized and professional

::: notes
Functions are helpful when trying to save time.
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Example Function


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
add_numbers <- function(x, y) {
  return(x + y)
}

add_numbers(3, 5)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] 8
```


:::
:::


------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Question 2: How do you use purr functions?

- when iteractions instead of loops
- best to use with tidyverse
- helps codes be shorter

## Example


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
library(purrr)

numbers <- list(1, 2, 3, 4)

squared <- map(numbers, ~ .x^2)

squared
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[[1]]
[1] 1

[[2]]
[1] 4

[[3]]
[1] 9

[[4]]
[1] 16
```


:::
:::


------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Question 3: What are some new things you learned that you didn't learn in Module 11?

- how functions can also be used inside other functions
- learned about anonymous functions, they are functions without names
- It helped me realize how important it is to plan codes

::: Key Insights - combine small functions, then combine them :::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Question 4: What did you like about the Quarto-based Interactive dashboard compared with other options introduced in this class previously?

- I liked that you can create interactive dashboards directly from Quarto because everything is from 1 file and it's easier/cleaner workflow.
- It doesn't need a lot of work and it looks professional
- Quarto is more integrated compared to the other tools we have used before.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Question 5: Explain what parameterized reporting is and how it works. Do you see the application of it in your workflow in your current or past jobs (or future jobs)?

- Parameterized reporting is a report that can change based on the input values, you can also create multiple reports automatically. It helps save time and can be helpful in jobs.

## Example:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
params <- list(name = "Student")

paste("Hello", params$name)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "Hello Student"
```


:::
:::


------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Question 6: What are his suggestions for ways in which you can make your dashboard effective and help your colleagues pay attention to your report?

- making it simple
- not adding too much information, too much makes it look messy and confsuing
- highlight key insights
- it used clear visuals
- try to tell a story with the data rather than just showing it
- if the dashboard is too confusing, people won't use it

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Question 7: Share some of the challenges you might have in learning the various tools in Modules 11 and 12.

- This class in gernal is challenging.
- understanding the functions and codes is difficult because you need to remember what they're used for and there's a lot
- debugging errors!!! It's super frustrating, google and AI sometimes can't even help.
- I tried to improve by watching the lecture videos and practing to understand better.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Question 8: Share your thoughts about the learning experience you have had. Also, share your plan if any of using the knowledge in the era of AI.

- Overall, I did learn a lot in these modules and I feel like I will use these skills in the real world. At first it was difficult, but a lot of practice will help become better.

include-after-body:
<script>
  // htmlwidgets need to know to resize themselves when slides are shown/hidden.
  // Fire the "slideenter" event (handled by htmlwidgets.js) when the current
  // slide changes (different for each slide format).
  (function () {
    // dispatch for htmlwidgets
    function fireSlideEnter() {
      const event = window.document.createEvent("Event");
      event.initEvent("slideenter", true, true);
      window.document.dispatchEvent(event);
    }

    function fireSlideChanged(previousSlide, currentSlide) {
      fireSlideEnter();

      // dispatch for shiny
      if (window.jQuery) {
        if (previousSlide) {
          window.jQuery(previousSlide).trigger("hidden");
        }
        if (currentSlide) {
          window.jQuery(currentSlide).trigger("shown");
        }
      }
    }

    // hookup for slidy
    if (window.w3c_slidy) {
      window.w3c_slidy.add_observer(function (slide_num) {
        // slide_num starts at position 1
        fireSlideChanged(null, w3c_slidy.slides[slide_num - 1]);
      });
    }

  })();
</script>
